did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning and machine learning models, specifically CNN + Random Forest, DenseNet201, MobileNetV2, and ConvoMixer + CatBoost, to classify skin cancer (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, etc.) using datasets like HAM10000, ISIC, and PAD-UFES-20.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results, including the development and evaluation of specific deep learning models (CNN + Random Forest, DenseNet201, MobileNetV2, ConvoMixer + CatBoost) on datasets like HAM10000, ISIC, and PAD-UFES-20. It includes a methodology section detailing model architectures, training procedures, and a results section reporting accuracy metrics (e.g., 96% for CNN+RF), which characterizes it as an original technical study rather than a review.

title: Optimizing Skin Cancer Detection Through Hybrid Convolutional and Ensemble Learning Methods
journal: 2025 IEEE International Students' Conference on Electrical, Electronics, and Computer Science
brief_summary: This study evaluates the performance of several deep learning and hybrid models for skin cancer classification using the HAM10000, ISIC, and PAD-UFES-20 datasets. The researchers assessed CNN + Random Forest, DenseNet201, MobileNetV2, and ConvoMixer + CatBoost architectures to automate the detection of melanoma and other skin lesions. Experimental results indicated that the CNN + Random Forest hybrid model achieved the highest accuracy of 96% on the HAM10000 dataset. In comparison, DenseNet201 reached 91.8% accuracy on the ISIC dataset, MobileNetV2 achieved 86% on HAM10000, and ConvoMixer + CatBoost obtained 66% on PAD-UFES-20. The findings demonstrate that combining CNN feature extraction with Random Forest classification offers superior performance for skin cancer detection compared to the other tested models.

lesion_types_studied.details: Identified as the most dangerous type of skin cancer in the abstract and introduction. Included in the HAM10000 dataset (class label 'mel': 4) and ISIC dataset.
Listed as one of the skin categories in the HAM10000 dataset (class label 'bcc': 1).
Mentioned as one of the seven distinct skin categories in the HAM10000 collection.
Included in the HAM10000 dataset class labels (class label 'akiec': 0).
Included in the HAM10000 dataset class labels (class label 'bkl': 2).
Included in the HAM10000 dataset class labels (class label 'df': 3).
Included in the HAM10000 dataset class labels (class label 'nv': 5).
Included in the HAM10000 dataset class labels (class label 'vasc': 6).
Referenced in the introduction as the majority of skin cancer cases and in the methodology as part of the ISIC dataset composition.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer diagnostic categories (e.g., melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis, benign keratosis, dermatofibroma, nevus, vascular lesion)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly focuses on 'skin cancer classification' and 'categorization of skin cancer'. Models predict discrete class labels (e.g., 'akiec': 0, 'bcc': 1, etc.) using softmax activation and categorical crossentropy loss. The task involves distinguishing between multiple benign and malignant skin lesion types.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A publicly accessible dataset containing more than 10,000 dermatoscopic images divided into seven distinct skin categories. It is described as one of the biggest and most popular datasets for classifying skin lesions.

use.training: Used for training MobileNetV2 and CNN + Random Forest models.
use.validation: Used for validation (specifically mentioned for MobileNetV2 training process).
use.testing: Used for testing/evaluation of MobileNetV2 and CNN + Random Forest models.

datasets_used.name: ISIC
datasets_used.description: The International Skin Imaging Collaboration collection, described as having thousands of annotated photos of different skin conditions, including melanoma and non-melanoma cases. It is frequently used for benchmarking machine learning models in dermatology research.

use.testing: Used for testing/evaluation of DenseNet201 model.

datasets_used.name: PAD-UFES-20
datasets_used.description: A publicly accessible dataset used in the study. The paper notes that for the ConvoMixer + CatBoost model, this dataset was enriched with metadata (patient age, gender, and lesion type) alongside image data.

use.training: Used for training ConvoMixer + CatBoost model (with metadata integration).
use.testing: Used for testing/evaluation of ConvoMixer + CatBoost model.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated and compared using accuracy metrics only.

HAM10000.notes: CNN + Random Forest outperformed MobileNetV2 on this dataset. MobileNetV2 was fine-tuned using pre-trained ImageNet weights.

ISIC.notes: DenseNet201 was applied as a feature extractor leveraging pre-trained ImageNet weights. The dataset included melanoma and non-melanoma cases.

PAD-UFES-20.notes: This model combined image data with metadata (patient age, gender, and lesion type). The paper notes it did not perform as strongly as other models, indicating a need for refinement in handling multi-modal inputs.

architecture_used.main_model: Four models evaluated: CNN + Random Forest, DenseNet201, MobileNetV2, and ConvoMixer + CatBoost.
architecture_used.pretraining: DenseNet201 and MobileNetV2 utilized pre-trained ImageNet weights. CNN and ConvoMixer pretraining details not explicitly reported.
architecture_used.input_size: CNN: [28, 28, 3]; DenseNet201: Resized to (75, 100, 3); MobileNetV2: Input shape not explicitly stated but processed from HAM10000; ConvoMixer: Input shape [H, W, C] with patch size P.

DenseNet201.type: Deep Convolutional Network with dense connectivity
DenseNet201.modifications: Flattened output to 1D vector, added dropout (p=0.5), added dense layer with ReLU, added softmax output layer.

MobileNetV2.modifications: Excluded last 5 layers, flattened output, added dense layer (1024 units, ReLU), added dropout (0.25), added output layer (7 units, softmax).

components: ConvMixer Blocks (Depthwise Conv KxK, BatchNorm, ReLU)

DenseNet201.learning_rate_schedule: Reduced if no improvement in validation top-3 accuracy for 2 consecutive epochs; new rate = alpha * factor

MobileNetV2.learning_rate_schedule: Reduced if no improvement in validation top-3 accuracy for 2 consecutive epochs

classification_or_output_head.DenseNet201: Dense layer with ReLU activation followed by softmax-activated output layer with NumClasses units
classification_or_output_head.MobileNetV2: Dense layer (1024 units, ReLU) -> Dropout (0.25) -> Output layer (7 units, softmax)
classification_or_output_head.ConvoMixer: Dense layer with C units and softmax activation
classification_or_output_head.Random_Forest: Ensemble of decision trees using majority voting
classification_or_output_head.CatBoost: Gradient boosting with ordered boosting, target statistics for categorical features

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing: Images reshaped to dimensions (75, 100, 3) for DenseNet201; input shape [28, 28, 3] for CNN; patch division of size P for ConvoMixer.
Normalization: Z-score normalization applied to training and testing sets for DenseNet201 (xtrain = (xtrain − mean(xtrain))/std(xtrain)).
Data Augmentation: Rotation, scaling, and flipping applied for MobileNetV2 to balance class distributions and enhance generalization.
Duplicate Removal: Duplicate images deleted for MobileNetV2 to ensure data quality.
Dimensionality Reduction: Principal Component Analysis (PCA) used for feature space reduction in the ConvoMixer + CatBoost pipeline to address the curse of dimensionality.
Label Encoding: Labels converted to one-hot encoded format for DenseNet201.
Class Weighting: Class weights applied for DenseNet201 to handle imbalanced classes (e.g., melanoma weight 3.0, others 1.0).
Metadata Integration: Patient age, gender, and lesion type metadata combined with image features for ConvoMixer + CatBoost.
Categorical Feature Encoding: Target statistics used for encoding categorical features in CatBoost.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the MobileNetV2 dataset to balance class distributions and enhance generalization. The dataset size increased to 38,500 images after augmentation.
Used for DenseNet201 to handle imbalanced classes. Weights were set to 1.0 for classes 'akiec', 'bcc', 'bkl', 'df', 'nv', and 'vasc', and 3.0 for class 'mel' (melanoma).

conclusions: The hybrid CNN + Random Forest model achieved the highest accuracy of 96% on the HAM10000 dataset, outperforming all other tested models and demonstrating the effectiveness of hybrid approaches for skin cancer classification.
DenseNet201 achieved an accuracy of 91.8% on the ISIC dataset, while MobileNetV2 achieved 86% on the HAM10000 dataset.

The ConvoMixer + CatBoost model, which integrated image data with patient metadata (age, gender, lesion type), achieved only 66% accuracy on the PAD-UFES-20 dataset, indicating that handling multi-modal inputs remains a challenge requiring further refinement.
While DenseNet201 and MobileNetV2 performed well, they involved trade-offs regarding runtime efficiency compared to the hybrid CNN + Random Forest approach.
Limitations acknowledged include the complexity of metadata integration and high computational requirements, which negatively impacted the performance of the multi-modal model.

Future work should focus on balancing datasets, advancing multi-modal learning techniques, and exploring transformer-based models (e.g., Vision Transformers, Swin Transformers) and federated learning to improve accuracy, interpretability, and privacy.
important_remarks: The study is a conference paper from a student conference (SCEECS 2025), which may indicate limited peer review depth compared to major journals.
Performance metrics are reported exclusively as accuracy; critical metrics for imbalanced medical datasets such as sensitivity, specificity, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC-ROC are not reported.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the differences in model performance.

The ConvoMixer + CatBoost model achieved a notably low accuracy of 66% on the PAD-UFES-20 dataset, suggesting potential issues with the multi-modal integration or dataset suitability for that specific architecture.
The CNN + Random Forest model achieved the highest accuracy (96%) on HAM10000, but the paper does not report standard deviation or variance across multiple runs, limiting the reliability assessment.

Class imbalance was addressed via class weighting (melanoma weight 3.0) for DenseNet201, but the specific impact of this weighting on false negative rates for the most dangerous class (melanoma) is not detailed.

Data leakage risks are not explicitly addressed; specifically, it is unclear if duplicate images were removed prior to train/test split for all models, although MobileNetV2 preprocessing mentions duplicate removal.
The study relies on publicly available datasets (HAM10000, ISIC, PAD-UFES-20) without external validation on independent clinical cohorts, limiting generalizability to real-world clinical settings.

The paper acknowledges that hybrid approaches trade off runtime efficiency for accuracy, but does not provide computational complexity metrics (e.g., FLOPs, inference time) to quantify this trade-off.
